findings.problem: An unexpected control-timer backend failure bypasses the reviewed Phase B fail-safe latch semantics. Instead of preserving a runtime fault as `FAIL_SAFE_LATCHED`, the failure handler clears the control state back to `DISARMED`, which erases the required explicit re-arm boundary for an interrupted run and leaves the product loop alive with no active control timer.
findings.evidence: Phase B requires a runtime fault interrupting `SPINUP`, `RUNNING`, or `STOPPING` to enter and remain in `FAIL_SAFE_LATCHED` until explicit re-arm conditions are satisfied: `openspec/changes/archive/2026-04-19-implement-phase-b-motion-lifecycle/specs/phase-b-motion-lifecycle/spec.md:89-106`. The timer failure path only emits diagnostics, disables the motor, and calls `ResetControlState()`: `new/code/runtime/control_loop.cpp:482-495`. `ResetControlState()` unconditionally sets `state_.motion_state.phase = MotionPhase::kDisarmed`: `new/code/runtime/control_loop.cpp:497-505`. The main product loop only recognizes post-fault reset automation when the published phase is `FAIL_SAFE_LATCHED`, and otherwise keeps running until `stop_requested` is set elsewhere: `new/user/main.cpp:344-409`.
findings.recommendation: Treat timer-backend runtime failure as a project-owned runtime fault, not as a clean disarm. Preserve a latched motion-fault state and corresponding reset-ready semantics, and either request a controlled process shutdown or introduce a reviewed recovery path that restores timer ownership before motion may restart. Do not collapse this path to `DISARMED`.
